questions.to_find: What values are printed for i and j after the function call f(&i, &j)

understand.plain: The function f receives pointers to i and j. Inside f, the statement p=q makes the local pointer p point to j (not to i). Then *p=2 sets the value at the address p points to (which is j) to 2. Since p and q are local copies of the pointers, the change to p does not affect i at all. So i remains 0 and j becomes 2.

keywords.explain: This copies the address stored in q into p. It changes where p points, but does NOT change the variable that p originally pointed to.
keywords.example: If p points to i and q points to j, after p=q both p and q point to j. i is untouched.

keywords.explain: This writes the value 2 to the memory location p currently points to.
keywords.example: After p=q, *p=2 sets j=2, not i.

keywords.explain: Passing &i means the function receives the address of i. Changes via *p affect the original variable, but reassigning p (the local copy of the address) does not.
keywords.example: p=q changes the local pointer; *p=2 changes what p now points to.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>i = 0</text><text x='50' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>addr: 100</text><rect x='140' y='20' width='80' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='180' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>j = 2</text><text x='180' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>addr: 200</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>p = 200</text><text x='50' y='142' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(was 100)</text><rect x='140' y='110' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>q = 200</text><text x='180' y='142' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>unchanged</text><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='180' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='180' y1='150' x2='180' y2='60' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><text x='240' y='145' class='sub ink'>p=q: p now points to j</text><text x='240' y='162' class='sub ink'>*p=2: j becomes 2</text><text x='240' y='179' class='sub ink'>i untouched: i=0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memory layout showing i=0 at address 100, j changes to 2 at address 200. After p=q inside f, local pointer p points to j. *p=2 sets j=2. i stays 0.

given.aim: Determine the output of i and j after calling f(&i, &j) where f reassigns the local pointer and then dereferences it.

terms.meaning: Global integer variables initialized to 0 and 1 respectively.
terms.example: i is at some address, say 100; j is at address 200.
terms.connects: These are the variables whose values we print at the end.

terms.meaning: Local pointer p is reassigned to hold the address of j.
terms.example: After this, p and q both hold address 200 (address of j).
terms.connects: This means subsequent *p operations affect j, not i.

terms.meaning: Sets the value at the address p currently points to (j) to 2.
terms.connects: i is untouched because p no longer points to i.

given.plan: Trace the pointer operations step by step: p=q redirects p from i to j; *p=2 sets j=2; i never changes. Print i=0, j=2.

steps.title: Function call: initial pointer values
steps.apply: f is called with f(&i, &j). Inside f, local pointer p holds address of i (say 100), and local pointer q holds address of j (say 200).
steps.note: p and q are local copies of the addresses; modifying p does not affect i directly.

steps.title: Execute p = q
steps.formula_raw: p = q => p now holds address of j
steps.apply: p is reassigned to hold address 200 (address of j). Now both p and q point to j. The variable i at address 100 is no longer pointed to by p.
steps.note: This is pointer reassignment, NOT *p = *q. The original variable i is completely unaffected.

steps.title: Execute *p = 2
steps.apply: Since p now points to j, *p=2 sets j=2. i remains 0.
steps.note: The key insight: p=q changed WHERE p points, so *p=2 modifies j, not i.

steps.title: Back in main: print i and j
steps.apply: i=0 (never modified), j=2 (set inside f). Output: 0 2

questions.formula_note: Pointer reassignment (p=q) changes the local pointer's target; dereferencing (*p=2) then modifies the new target. The original variable pointed to by p before reassignment is unaffected.

questions.to_find: The return value of f(x, c) with x=5 and c=5 (first parameter by reference, second by value)

understand.plain: The function f is recursive. x is passed by reference (all recursive calls share the same x variable), while c is passed by value (each call gets its own copy). Each recursive call decrements c by 1 and increments x by 1 (via the shared reference). When c reaches 0, the recursion stops and returns 1. On the way back up, each frame multiplies f(x,c)*x, but x is now the final shared value (9) at the time of unwinding.

keywords.explain: The function receives the memory address of x. Any change to x inside the function changes the original variable. All recursive calls see the same x.
keywords.example: x=5 initially; after 4 recursive increments x=9; all frames see x=9 when returning.

keywords.explain: The function receives a copy of c. Changes to c inside one call do not affect c in the calling frame.
keywords.example: c=5 in the first call; each recursive call starts with its own copy decremented by 1.

keywords.explain: After the base case returns, each frame executes its return statement multiplying the result by x. Since x is shared and has reached its final value, all frames see the same x.
keywords.example: Each of the 4 returning frames multiplies by x=9, giving 9^4=6561.

understand.visual_alt: Recursion tree: f(x,5) -> f(x,4) -> f(x,3) -> f(x,2) -> f(x,1) returns 1. x increments from 5 to 9 across calls. On unwind: 1*9 * 9 * 9 * 9 = 6561.

given.aim: Trace the recursive function with x (by reference) starting at 5 and c (by value) starting at 5, and find the return value.

terms.meaning: x is a shared variable; every recursive call modifies the same x.
terms.example: After 4 calls each doing x=x+1, x becomes 9.
terms.connects: When unwinding, all return statements use x=9.

terms.meaning: Each call gets its own copy of c, decremented by 1 at the start.
terms.example: Call 1 has c=4 after decrement, call 2 has c=3, ..., call 5 has c=0 (base case).
terms.connects: c controls the recursion depth: 4 recursive calls before base case.

terms.meaning: Each frame returns the product of the recursive call result and the current x.
terms.example: Since x is by reference and final value is 9, each frame contributes a factor of 9.

given.plan: Trace each recursive call: c decrements, x increments (shared). Base case at c=0 returns 1. On unwinding, multiply by x (which is 9 by then) four times.

steps.title: Trace recursive calls (descent)
steps.formula_raw: Each call: c_new = c-1, x_new = x+1 (shared)
steps.apply:
Call 1: c=5->4, x=5->6. Calls f(x,4).
Call 2: c=4->3, x=6->7. Calls f(x,3).
Call 3: c=3->2, x=7->8. Calls f(x,2).
Call 4: c=2->1, x=8->9. Calls f(x,1).
Call 5: c=1->0. Base case: return 1.
steps.note: After all descending calls, x = 9 (incremented 4 times from 5).

steps.title: Unwinding: compute return values
steps.formula_raw: return f(x,c) * x where x=9 at unwind time
steps.apply:
Call 4 returns: 1 * x = 1 * 9 = 9
Call 3 returns: 9 * x = 9 * 9 = 81
Call 2 returns: 81 * x = 81 * 9 = 729
Call 1 returns: 729 * x = 729 * 9 = 6561
steps.note: Because x is passed by reference, all frames see x=9 (the final value) when multiplying on return.

solution.result: The return value of f(x, 5) with x initialized to 5 is 6561.

questions.formula_note: Pass-by-reference means all recursive frames share the same variable x. After 4 increments x=9. On unwinding, 4 frames each multiply by 9: result = 9^4 = 6561.

questions.to_find: The value printed by the program after calling mystery(&a, &b) and the conditional update to b

understand.plain: The function mystery receives pointers to a and b, and swaps the local pointer variables ptra and ptrb. However, since the pointers themselves are passed by value, swapping the local copies does not affect the actual variables a and b in main. So a remains 2016 and b remains 0. Then since a=2016 is NOT less than c=4, the else branch executes: b = b - d = 0 - 42 = -42.

keywords.explain: Inside mystery, ptra and ptrb are local copies of the pointer arguments. Swapping them changes which local variable holds which address, but has no effect outside the function.
keywords.example: After mystery(&a,&b): a=2016 and b=0 are unchanged.

keywords.explain: Even though we pass pointers, the pointers themselves are passed by value. Reassigning the pointer variable (not dereferencing) affects only the local copy.
keywords.example: To actually swap a and b, we would need to dereference: *ptra=..., not ptra=...

understand.visual_alt: mystery() receives copies of &a and &b. Swapping ptra and ptrb locally does not affect a or b. After return: a=2016, b=0. Since 2016 is not < 4, else executes: b=0-42=-42.

given.aim: Determine if mystery() actually swaps a and b, then evaluate the conditional to find the printed value.

terms.meaning: Initial values of local variables in main.
terms.example: a and b are passed by address to mystery.
terms.connects: After mystery (which does nothing to actual values), a=2016 and b=0.

terms.meaning: Only swaps local pointer copies. No dereferencing, so actual values of a and b are unchanged.
terms.example: temp=ptrb; ptrb=ptra; ptra=temp; — all local pointer operations.
terms.connects: a stays 2016, b stays 0 after the call.

terms.meaning: 2016 is not less than 4, so the else branch executes.

given.plan: Recognize that mystery only swaps local pointer copies (no dereferencing), so a and b are unchanged. Then evaluate 2016 < 4 (false), take else branch: b = 0 - 42 = -42.

steps.title: Analyze mystery() function
steps.formula_raw: temp=ptrb; ptrb=ptra; ptra=temp (local swap of pointer variables)
steps.apply: mystery receives copies of &a and &b. Inside, it only swaps the local pointer variables ptra and ptrb. There is no dereferencing (*ptra = ... or *ptrb = ...). Therefore, the actual variables a and b in main are completely unmodified.
steps.note: This is a classic trap: swapping pointer variables vs. swapping what they point to.

steps.title: State after mystery call
steps.apply: Since mystery does not change the values at the addresses, a=2016 and b=0 remain as initialized.
steps.note: If mystery had used *ptra and *ptrb, the values would have swapped.

steps.title: Evaluate conditional and compute output
steps.formula_raw: if (a < c) => if (2016 < 4) => false => else: b = b - d
steps.apply: 2016 < 4 is false. Execute else: b = b - d = 0 - 42 = -42. printf prints -42.

solution.result: The output of the program is -42 (Option A).

questions.formula_note: Swapping pointer variables inside a function (without dereferencing) has no effect on the original variables. Only dereferencing (*ptr = ...) modifies the pointed-to values.

questions.to_find: The value of i + j printed after calling f(&i, j)

understand.plain: The function f takes a pointer to i and a copy of j. Inside f, m (copy of j=10) is incremented to 15. Then *p (which is i) is updated: i = i + m = 5 + 15 = 20. j is passed by value so j remains 10 in main. The printf prints i + j = 20 + 10 = 30.

keywords.explain: f receives the address of i. *p=*p+m modifies i directly in main.
m is a copy of j. Changing m inside f does not affect j in main.
keywords.example: m=j=10 initially; m becomes 15 inside f; j stays 10 in main.

understand.visual_alt: f(&i, j): p points to i (=5), m is copy of j (=10). m becomes 15. *p = 5+15 = 20, so i=20. j is unchanged at 10. i+j = 30.

given.aim: Compute i + j after f(&i, j) modifies i via pointer while j remains unchanged.

terms.meaning: Local variables in main with initial values.
terms.example: i will be modified via pointer; j will not be modified.
terms.connects: Final answer is i+j after the function call.

terms.meaning: m is a local copy of j (=10). After m=m+5, m=15.
terms.example: This does not change j in main.

terms.meaning: Dereferences p (which points to i) and sets i = i + m = 5 + 15 = 20.
terms.example: i is now 20 after this operation.
terms.connects: i=20 contributes to the final sum i+j.

given.plan: Trace the function: m becomes 15 (local copy), i is updated via pointer to 5+15=20, j stays 10. Compute i+j = 30.

steps.title: Function f called with f(&i, j): f(&5_addr, 10)
steps.apply: Inside f: p points to i (value 5), m is a local copy of j=10.
steps.note: m is by value; changes to m do not affect j in main.

steps.title: Execute m = m + 5
steps.apply: m is now 15. j in main is still 10.
steps.note: Only the local copy m changes.

steps.title: Execute *p = *p + m
steps.apply: p points to i. *p (which is i) = 5 + 15 = 20. So i is now 20 in main.
steps.note: i is modified via pointer; this change persists in main.

steps.title: Compute and print i + j
steps.apply: Back in main: i=20, j=10 (unchanged). printf("%d", i+j) prints 30.

questions.formula_note: Pass-by-pointer allows the function to modify the caller's variable (i becomes 20). Pass-by-value protects j from modification. Final sum i+j = 20+10 = 30.
